coredata.dc:title: A sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe based on N/S doped carbon dots and mercaptoacetic acid capped CdTe quantum dots for the highly selective detection of multiple tetracycline antibiotics in food
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Yao
Shen, Lu
Liu, Yaqi
Hu, Ying
Long, Wanjun
Fu, Haiyan
She, Yuanbin
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S doped carbon dots (N/S-CQDs) and mercaptoacetic acid capped CdTe quantum dots (TGA-CdTe QDs) with sensitized and self-calibration functions was constructed to sensitively detect multiple tetracycline antibiotics (TCs). N/S-CQDs could attach stably to TGA-CdTe QDs and form a new composite ratiometric fluorescent probe that had a more than tenfold increase in sensitivity to TCs compared with each single QD. The probe could detect four common TCs as the color of the probe changed from bright red to dark red, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.47 × 10−2–1.78 × 10−2 mg/L. Practical applications of the probe in food and urine were also verified with recovery rates of 95.21%-104.97%. Due to the abundant spectral fingerprints provided by both QDs, this novel probe could accurately recognize not only different single TCs but also mixed TC samples even in actual samples combined with chemometrics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Quantum dots
Sensitized effect
Tetracycline antibiotics
Mixed sample recognition
Chemometrics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs
Preparation of ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs
Characterization of sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe based on N/S-CQDS and TGA-CdTe QDs
Optimization of the detection conditions for TCs
Sensitivity of the sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe
Specificity and selectivity of the sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe
Stability of the sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe
Detection of TCs in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM characterization of N/S-CQDs; (B) TEM characterization of TGA-CdTe QDs; (C) TEM characterization of ratiometric fluorescent probe. (D) UV–Vis spectra of N/S-CQDs, TGA-CdTe QDs, and ratiometric fluorescent probe before and after adding TCs; (E) FT-IR spectra of ratiometric fluorescent probe before and after adding TCs.

The fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric fluorescent probe in the presence of different concentrations of OTC (A), TC (B), CTC (C) and DOX (D). Inset: Calibration curve of I414/I615 and concentrations of OTC, TC, CTC and DOX. (E) Visual fluorescence of the ratiometric fluorescent probe after the addition of TCs (0–2.00 × 10-5 mol/L) under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Discrimination result of the fluorescence response for multiple tetracycline antibiotics (total concentration was set as 3 × 10-6 mol/L (OTC 1.38 mg/L, TC 1.33 mg/L, CTC 1.45 mg/L and DOX 1.33 mg/L) while the antibiotics in the mixture were mixed in equal concentrations, A) and its practical application (B).

(A) The specificity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe toward different kinds of antibiotics (1.0 × 10-4 mol/L); (B) The selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe toward common metal ions and small biological molecules (1.0 × 10-3 mol/L).
Schematic diagram of the ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs for the detection of multiple TCs.
Comparison of various TC detection methods.

head.ce:title: A sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe based on N/S doped carbon dots and mercaptoacetic acid capped CdTe quantum dots for the highly selective detection of multiple tetracycline antibiotics in food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S doped carbon dots (N/S-CQDs) and mercaptoacetic acid capped CdTe quantum dots (TGA-CdTe QDs) with sensitized and self-calibration functions was constructed to sensitively detect multiple tetracycline antibiotics (TCs). N/S-CQDs could attach stably to TGA-CdTe QDs and form a new composite ratiometric fluorescent probe that had a more than tenfold increase in sensitivity to TCs compared with each single QD. The probe could detect four common TCs as the color of the probe changed from bright red to dark red, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.47 × 10−2–1.78 × 10−2 mg/L. Practical applications of the probe in food and urine were also verified with recovery rates of 95.21%-104.97%. Due to the abundant spectral fingerprints provided by both QDs, this novel probe could accurately recognize not only different single TCs but also mixed TC samples even in actual samples combined with chemometrics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Known as widely produced and used antibiotics, tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) are widely used in medical treatment, animal husbandry and aquaculture due to their good antibacterial performance, low cost, few side effects and good therapeutic effect (He, Ma, Yang, Hemar, & Zhao, 2020; Lanjwani, Altunay, & Tuzen, 2023). However, TCs are difficult to degrade and may accumulate in the soil, water and foods such as meat, eggs and milk due to their stable structures, which further endangers human health and the ecological environment through the food chain and ecological cycle (Oluwole & Olatunji, 2022; Wu, Yang, Lin, & Cheng, 2022). Furthermore, TCs, including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC) and doxycycline (DOX), have similar structures but obvious regional and environmental distribution differences in residue (Li, Pan, Miao, Chen, & Tao, 2015). Therefore, an efficient probe for the detection of multiple tetracycline antibiotics is of significant importance to improve food safety and protect human beings from antibiotic contamination.

In recent years, several methods, including high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (Pang, Lv, Sun, Yang, & Shen, 2021), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Miranda, Rodríguez, & Galán-Vidal, 2009) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) (Le, Zhao, Wei, & Bi, 2012), have been continuously developed for the sensitive detection of TCs. However, these methods often require high-cost instruments, are complex and time-consuming pretreatment, and require experienced technicians. On the other hand, fluorescent methods, especially fluorescent probes based on quantum dots (QDs), have attracted extensive attention because of their high sensitivity and potential visualization applications (Fan, Zhang, Jia, Chen, Fu, & She, 2020; Wang et al., 2022). Meanwhile, when faced with the recognition of analytes with highly similar structures such as TCs, traditional fluorescent probes with just a single characteristic peak may have difficulty achieving the ideal effect of simultaneous detection and are susceptible to interference from other factors (Chen, Xu, Yu, & Wang, 2018; Fan et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2018). Aiming at the above issues, ratiometric fluorescence probes with more abundant spectra and good self-correction ability are reasonable for the highly selective detection of multiple TCs (Yang, Jia, Hou, Zhao, & Wang, 2022). QD-based ratiometric fluorescent probes have been widely used in the detection of a variety of analytes, including metal ions (Qian et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017), tyrosinase (Qu, Yu, & Bi, 2019), RNA (G. Li, Liu, Niu, Pei, & Lin, 2018), small biological molecules (Mi et al., 2021; Shi, Zhang, Yu, Liu, & Chen, 2019), and real food samples (Hu et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2019), and could significantly improve the sensitivity and selectivity. However, based on the available literature, most ratiometric fluorescent probes combine the spectral features of two materials without sensitization.

In particular, carbon dots (CQDs) have the advantages of simple synthesis and abundant surface group modification (Lin & Zhang, 2012; Pei, Zhang, Gao, Wu, Yang, & Liu, 2015; Zhou et al., 2016), while CdTe QDs have excellent photoelectric properties and high quantum yield (B. Chen et al., 2019; Sahoo, Mandal, Mitra, Chakraborty, Bardhan, & Dasgupta, 2018; Zhang et al., 2017). By modifying -SH and –NH2 on the surface of CQDs to form N/S-doped CQDs (N/S-CQDs), -SH could directly bind to the surface of CdTe QDs, while –NH2 could combine with –COOH in mercaptoacetic acid (TGA) capped on CdTe QDs. The stable combination of two QDs was further adopted to construct a new composite ratiometric fluorescent probe with greater binding ability and sensitization effect with TCs than each single QD. Therefore, the as-prepared ratiometric fluorescent probe was considered to be an ideal tool for the detection of multiple tetracycline antibiotics in food due to the richer spectral information and greater sensitivity.

In this study, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs with sensitized and self-calibration functions was constructed for the detection of multiple TCs in food (Scheme 1 ). The composite probe not only possessed the properties of both QDs but also had a more than tenfold increase in sensitivity to TCs compared with each single QD. In addition, the probe could achieve accurate quantitative analysis of four common TCs, including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC) and doxycycline (DOX), in food and urine matrices with obvious visualizations. Moreover, by combining it with a linear discriminant analysis (LDA) model, the composite probe could accurately recognize both different single TCs and mixed samples, including two and three TCs, even in actual samples. The construction of the sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe provided a new research basis for the design and efficiency enhancement of the ratiometric fluorescence probe and had guiding significance for its development and application.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA), sodium acetate (NaAC), acetic acid (HAC), sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), aluminum chloride (AlCl3), sodium chloride (NaCl), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), cobalt chloride (CoCl2) and nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Sulfamethoxazole (SMZ), oxytetracycline (OTC) and penicillin sodium (PG) were purchased from Shanghai Anaiji Chemical Co., Ltd. Streptomycin sulfate (STR), kanamycin monosulfate (KAN), neomycin sulfate (NEO), and chloramphenicol (CHL) were purchased from Shanghai Lianshuo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Chlortetracycline (CTC), doxycycline (DOX), roxithromycin (RXM), sulfapyridine sodium (SM2), thioglycolic acid (TGA), azithromycin (AZT), erythromycin (ERY), tetracycline (TC), enrofloxacin (ENR), gentamicin sulfate (GEN), clarithromycin (CCR), artificial urine and all amino acids, including glucose (Glu), reduced glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (AA), histidine (His), serine (Ser), cysteine (Cys), phenylalanine (Phe), threonine (Thr), theanine (The) and aspartic acid (Asp), were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd.

The morphology and size of N/S-CQDs and TCA-CdTe QDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI-F20, USA). The fluorescence spectra were recorded by an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Tech Company, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime was measured by an FLS980 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, Britain). The UV–Vis spectra were recorded by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (U-3900) (Hitachi High-Tech Company, Japan). The infrared spectra were measured by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Nicolet, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs

ce:section.ce:para: According to the method reported by our laboratory (Fan et al., 2022), 0.2101 g of citric acid and 0.7683 g of reduced glutathione were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The solution was cooled to room temperature and further filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The as-prepared N/S-CQDs were transferred to a 3500 Da dialysis bag and preserved in ultrapure water for 24 h in the dark. The ultrapure water was changed every 6 h to obtain pure N/S-CQDs. Finally, the obtained N/S-CQDs were diluted with ultrapure water 1000 times and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

The preparation of TGA-CdTe QDs was slightly modified by the reported method (Fan, Zhang, Jia, Chen, Fu, & She, 2020). A total of 0.2291 g of cadmium chloride and 0.1152 g of thioglycolic acid were dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water, stirred for 15 min, adjusted to pH 11.0 and purged with nitrogen for 20 min in an ice bath. Then, 0.0554 g of Na2TeO3 was added into the solution and sequentially stirred for 15 min. Finally, 0.0284 g of NaBH4 was added into the solution and stirred for 15 min, and then the solution was placed in the reaction kettle and hydrothermally reacted at 180 ℃ for 50 min. The obtained TGA-CdTe QD solution was further filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The solution was then transferred to a 3500 Da dialysis bag and preserved in ultrapure water for 24 h in the dark. The ultrapure water was changed every 6 h to obtain pure QDs. The as-prepared TGA-CdTe QD solution was finally diluted 100 times with ultrapure water as a stock solution and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N/S-CQDs synthesized in 1) and the TGA-CdTe QDs synthesized in 2) were mixed together in a volume ratio of 1:1.5 for 20 mins, and the ratiometric fluorescent probe was obtained and stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃.

Under an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the fluorescence emission peaks of the ratiometric fluorescent probes based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs are 414 nm and 580 nm, respectively. Both the excitation and emission slit widths are set to 10 nm. The fluorescence determination method of tetracycline antibiotics is as follows: 100 μL ratiometric fluorescent probe stock solution was added to 800 μL NaAC-HAC buffer solution (50 mM, pH = 7.0) and then mixed with 100 μL different concentrations of TCs for 10 mins. The measurement of the fluorescence spectra was performed using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectrum data in the range of 360 ∼ 670 nm under an excitation wavelength of 340 nm were recorded with the width of slit set as 10 nm-10 nm and a voltage of 700 V. Each kind of TC was set to 10 different concentrations during the quantitative analysis, and each concentration was tested three times in parallel.

A 100 μL ratiometric fluorescent probe stock solution, 800 μL NaAC-HAC buffer solution (50 mM, pH = 7.0), and 100 μL antibiotics were mixed together for 10 min in the dark. The concentration of TCs, including TC, OTC, CTC and DOX, used in the selectivity study was 1.20 × 10-5 mol/L (5.33 mg/L, 5.53 mg/L, 5.75 mg/L and 5.33 mg/L, respectively), while the concentration of other antibiotics was 1.0 × 10-4 mol/L. The measurement of the fluorescence spectra was performed under the same conditions as in Section 2.4.

A 100 μL ratiometric fluorescent probe stock solution, 800 μL NaAC-HAC buffer solution (50 mM, pH = 7.0), and 100 μL interference (1.0 × 10-3 mol/L), such as meta ions or amino acids, were mixed for 10 min in the dark. The measurement of the fluorescence spectra was performed under the same conditions as in Section 2.4.

The milk sample was prepared as follows: First, 5 mL of pure milk containing TCs at various concentrations was mixed with 20 mL of McIlvaine buffer (pH 5.0) containing 20 mmol/L EDTA. Then, the milk sample was degreased by adding 2 mL trichloroacetic acid and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min. Then, by adding NaOH, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.2. Finally, the milk sample was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane before use. Honey samples were prepared as follows: First, 5 g honey was pretreated with 25 mL PBS-EDTA (pH 7.2) to remove metal ions such as Ca2+ and Zn2+. Then, TCs at various concentrations were spiked into the honey solution for further use. The urine sample was filtered through a microporous membrane and spiked with various concentrations of TCs. Each kind of TC was set to 3 different concentrations during the practical food analysis, and each concentration was tested three times in parallel.

To verify the reliability of the sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe test results in real samples, the statistical p value obtained by the T test method provided by Excel software was adopted to analyze the difference between the fluorescence probe analysis results and HPLC analysis results. Accordingly, P greater than 0.05 was defined as no significant difference between two sets of data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe based on N/S-CQDS and TGA-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and particle size of the N/S-CQDs, TGA-CdTe QDs and composite ratiometric fluorescent probe were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A-C, N/S -CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs had good dispersibility and spherical morphology with a size of 3 nm. However, for the ratiometric fluorescence probe based on N/S-CQDS and TGA-CdTe QDs, two QDs exhibited an obvious combination, and the composite particle size was significantly increased to 8 nm. Through the UV–Vis shown in Fig. 1D, there was no obvious peak shift and a new absorption peak after the two QDs were mixed, indicating that no new conjugated system was formed. To explore the surface groups of the composite ratiometric fluorescent probe, FT-IR spectra were obtained, and the results are shown in Fig. 1D. The peaks appearing at approximately 2427 cm−1 and 3418 cm−1 corresponded to -SH and –NH2 stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1627 ∼ 1650 cm−1 were the absorption band of the CO stretching vibration, which was attributed to the characteristic peak of carboxyl groups (Jia et al., 2023). The FT-IR spectra showed that the surface of the composite ratiometric fluorescent probes mainly contained abundant carboxyl and amino functional groups. Moreover, after the addition of TCs, the characteristic peak intensity of the composite ratiometric fluorescent probe was significantly decreased, and the peak at approximately 2427 cm−1 disappeared, revealing that all the above functional groups participated in the combination with TCs.

The fluorescent responses of N/S-CQDs, TGA-CdTe QDs and the composite ratiometric fluorescent probe toward TCs were carefully investigated. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence spectra of both N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs before and after mixing did not change significantly, indicating that the composite ratiometric fluorescence still retained the fluorescence properties of the two QDs. Interestingly, after the addition of TCs (3.00 × 10-6 mol/L: (OTC 1.38 mg/L, TC 1.33 mg/L, CTC 1.45 mg/L and DOX 1.33 mg/L) to the composite ratiometric fluorescent probe, the fluorescence peak located at 410 nm, which belonged to the N/S-CQDs, retained the original response performance to TCs, while the fluorescence peak located at 615 nm, which belonged to the TGA-CdTe QDs, showed a more than tenfold increase in sensitivity to TCs. This may be due to different fluorescence quenching mechanisms. For the N/S-CQDs, the fluorescence was quenched by TCs through the internal filtration effect, so it could not be affected by the combination of two QDs (Fan et al., 2022). However, for the TGA-CdTe QDs, the fluorescence was quenched mainly depending on the electron transfer, which could be largely affected by the introduction of more functional groups, including –SH, –NH2 and –COOH, brought by N/S-CQDs. These functional groups further endowed the TGA-CdTe QDs with an enhanced electrostatic binding effect toward TCs, resulting in a more pronounced change and higher sensitivity of fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the detection conditions for TCs
ce:section.ce:para: First, the response time of the ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs toward TCs was optimized, and the results are shown in Fig. S2(A). After adding TCs to the ratiometric fluorescent probe, the I414/I615 values gradually increased over time. Herein, I414 is the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CQDs, while I615 is the fluorescence intensity of TGA-CdTe QDs. The I414/I615 value increased rapidly within 1 min and then rose slowly within 1–10 min and gradually stabilized after 10 min. Therefore, the reaction time was set as 10 min for the following study.

To ensure the sensitivity of the composite ratiometric fluorescent probe, TC was selected as the representative to explore the influence of pH on the fluorescent response of the ratiometric fluorescent probe during detection. As shown in Fig. S2B, I414/I615 showed the maximum value at pH = 7.0. This may be caused by the negative electricity contained on the surface of the composite ratiometric fluorescent probe being easily destroyed under overalkali conditions, while TCs were unstable in acidic conditions. Therefore, the NaAC-HAC buffer solution with pH = 7.0 was selected for the following study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe, different concentrations of TCs were added under the same conditions, and the fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric fluorescent probe were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2 A-2D, as the concentration of TCs increased, the value of I414/I615 also increased progressively. For OTC, there was a good linear relationship between the OTC concentration range of 1.06 × 10−7–5.00 × 10−6 mol/L (0.05–0.23 mg/L) and I414/I615, and the linear correlation equation was calculated as I414/I615 = 0.93364 + 0.18979COTC (R2 = 0.991, the standard deviation for intercept and slope was 0.0047 and 0.0026). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 3.20 × 10−8 mol/L (1.47 × 10−2 mg/L) through S/N = 3 (N is the standard deviation, while S is the slope). For TC detection, there was a good linear relationship between the TC concentration range of 1.22 × 10−7-1.00 × 10−5 mol/L (0.05–0.44 mg/L) and I414/I615, and the linear correlation equation was calculated as I414/I615 = 0.81108 + 0.13313CTC (R2 = 0.996, the standard deviation for intercept and slope was 0.0043 and 0.0011). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 3.70 × 10−8 mol/L (1.64 × 10−2 mg/L). For CTC detection, there was a good linear relationship between the CTC concentration range of 1.25 × 10−7-1.00 × 10−5 mol/L (0.06–0.48 mg/L) and I414/I615, and the linear correlation equation was calculated as I414/I615 = 0.9296 + 0.12772CCTC (R2 = 0.996, the standard deviation for intercept and slope was 0.0033 and 0.0014). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 3.80 × 10−8 mol/L (1.72 × 10−2 mg/L). For DOX detection, there was a good linear relationship between the DOX concentration range of 1.32 × 10−7-1.00 × 10−5 mol/L (0.06–0.44 mg/L) and I414/I615, and the linear correlation equation was calculated as I414/I615 = 0.91825 + 0.09858CDOX (R2 = 0.996, the standard deviation for intercept and slope was 0.0023 and 0.0015). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 4.00 × 10−8 mol/L (1.78 × 10−2 mg/L). It is worth noting that the fluorescence visualization characteristic of the ratiometric fluorescent probe was also excellent, which could achieve obvious visual detection of TCs. As shown in Fig. 2E, the probe had a bright red color with a 365 nm UV lamp. After the addition of TCs with different concentrations ranging from 0 to 2.00 × 10−5 mol/L (OTC 9.21 mg/L, TC 8.89 mg/L, CTC 9.58 mg/L and DOX 8.89 mg/L), the ratiometric fluorescent probe showed a visual effect from bright red to dark red with reduced fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and selectivity of the sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: To study the specificity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe to TCs, the quenching effect of common antibiotics toward the probe was tested under the same conditions. The concentration of TCs was set as 1.20 × 10-5 mol/L (5.33 mg/L, 5.53 mg/L, 5.75 mg/L and 5.33 mg/L), while the concentration of other antibiotics was set as 1.0 × 10-4 mol/L. As shown in Fig. 4 A, I414/I615 only increased significantly after the addition of TCs. Even though the concentration of other antibiotics was approximately 10 times higher than that of TCs, they did not cause obvious changes toward the probe, indicating that the probe had strong specificity for the detection of TCs.

To explore the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe, common metal ions and small biological molecules were added to observe the changes. As shown in Fig. 4B, either the fluorescence property of the probe itself or after the addition of TCs, the I414/I615 value did not change significantly compared with the control sample even in the presence of a high concentration of interferants (1.0 × 10-3 mol/L), which was approximately 100 times higher than TCs (1.20 × 10-5 mol/L), showing the satisfactory selectivity and anti-interference ability of the sensitized ratio fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: To study the stability of the sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe, its fluorescence response toward TC under storage conditions of room temperature (25 ℃) and the refrigerator (4 ℃) was tested. For the storage condition of 25 ℃, the fluorescence response of the probe was gradually reduced by 10% until 10 days, while for the storage condition of 4 ℃, its fluorescence response toward TC remained stable (reduced by less than 10%) after 13 days, indicating that the stability of the sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe was preserved better in the refrigerator at 4 ℃.

The fluorescence lifetime and zeta potential of the ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs after adding TCs were tested, and the results are shown in Fig. S3. The original fluorescence lifetime of the ratiometric fluorescent probe was 21.75 ns, and it dropped to 8.65 ns after adding 3.00 × 10-6 mol/L TC (OTC 1.38 mg/L, TC 1.33 mg/L, CTC 1.45 mg/L and DOX 1.33 mg/L) due to the effect of dynamic quenching. In addition, as shown in Fig. S3B, the surface of the ratiometric fluorescent probe carried a negative charge. After adding TC, the potential changed from −18.3 mV to 34.0 mV, which further illustrated that the reaction between the ratiometric fluorescent probe and TC was mainly electron transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical application of ratiometric fluorescent probes in the detection of TCs, milk, honey and urine were selected as actual samples in this study, and different concentrations of TCs were added by the spike recovery method and verified by HPLC. As shown in Table S1, the recovery rate of OTC in milk, honey and urine was 95.92%-104.97%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) did not exceed 1.04%. The recovery rate of OTC in actual samples ranged from 95.33% to 103.53%, and the RSD did not exceed 0.95%. The recovery rate of CTC in actual samples was 95.21%-104.97%, and the RSD was no more than 2.00%. The recovery rate of DOX in actual samples was 95.45%-103.83%, and the RSD was no more than 1.62%. Moreover, by using the statistical P values obtained by the T test method to verify the reliability of the fluorescence method, the data obtained by the fluorescence and HPLC methods were found to be not significantly different from each other (P > 0.05). The results demonstrated that the ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs could realize the accurate detection of TCs even in actual samples.

The performance of the probe to recognize multiple TCs in actual samples was also tested. As shown in Fig. 3B, the ratiometric fluorescent probe could still recognize multiple TCs even in the urine matrix. The samples including single TCs and the mixture of two or three TCs were all clearly separated from each other without any overlap, which proved the potential value of the ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs in the analysis of multiple TCs in actual samples.

In this study, a sensitized ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N/S-CQDs and TGA-CdTe QDs was constructed for the highly sensitive detection of multiple TCs. Compared with each single QD, the composite probe had a tenfold increase in sensitivity to TCs. Furthermore, the probe could be applied to the quantitative analysis of four common TCs accomplished by the obvious color change. The sensitized ratiometric fluorescent probe had good selectivity, which endowed it with a wide range of practical applications, such as TC detection in food and urine matrices. Moreover, due to the abundant fingerprint spectral information provided by both QDs, this novel probe could accurately recognize both different single TCs and mixed samples, including two and three TCs, even in actual samples, combined with the LDA model, providing new guidance for the design and efficiency enhancement of the ratiometric fluorescence probe and its applications.

Yao Fan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Lu Shen: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yaqi Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Ying Hu: Methodology, Software, Writing – review & editing. Wanjun Long: Data curation, Formal analysis. Haiyan Fu: Validation, Supervision. Yuanbin She: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants No. 32122068, 31972164, 22208381), the Research Initiation Fund of Zhejiang University of Technology (No. 2020101005929), Scientific Research Project of Education Department of Zhejiang Province (No. GZ21461010001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136105.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
